summary:
The Battle of Ankara (July 20, 1402 AD) was a catastrophic defeat of the [[event:94|Ottoman Empire]] by [[event:2276|Timur's]] forces near the Anatolian city of Ankara, in which the Ottoman Sultan Bayezid I was captured — the only Ottoman sultan ever taken prisoner — and the Ottoman state plunged into a decade-long civil war that nearly destroyed it. Timur had invaded Anatolia in response to Bayezid's sheltering of Timur's enemies; the two armies met in a day-long battle in which Timur's forces, including war elephants and Anatolian Turkish vassal contingents who defected from Bayezid during the battle, decisively overwhelmed the Ottoman army.

Bayezid I died in Timurid captivity shortly after his capture, a humiliation that generated extensive medieval mythology — most famously the story (probably false) that Timur used Bayezid as a human footstool. The defeat revealed both the Ottoman Empire's dependence on its sultan's personal authority and the fragility of a state barely a century old. The subsequent 'Ottoman Interregnum' (1402–1413) saw Bayezid's sons fight a bitter civil war before Mehmed I reunified the state. Paradoxically, Timur's near-destruction of the Ottomans may have prolonged the life of the Byzantine Empire by several decades — the Ottomans, preoccupied with civil war and reconstruction, could not resume their siege of Constantinople until the 1440s. Timur died in 1405 while preparing to invade China, and without his personal genius the Timurid Empire rapidly fragmented, allowing the Ottomans to recover and resume their expansion.
